speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three of an interview with Ester Lebensold. Today is November 27, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about arriving in Bremen--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bremen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to a camp in  Bremen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sure. It was a camp before, because when we came, it was 500 girls, Hungarian girls, already. And from us was 300.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the camp right in the city of Bremen?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the city. And after they get-- after they-- they bombed the-- the camp, because we were working-- because what we find out was under our camp was an ammunition factory. And we came, everything was on fire. Three girls got killed. And a lot they saved from the-- they were sick. They saved a few, and three got sick. And now, they have a monument in Bremen. They send us tickets to go-- to come to Bremen. They pay for everything, for the hotels. I didn't go, because-- my cousin went-- oh, a lot of girls. They pay for the hotels. They pay for everything in Bremen. And the three girls, they made a monument for them-- they got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: And after, they send us to [? Oberheiden. ?] They liquidate our camp. It was a-- I think-- I think it was from the French prisoners. And they have to give us a place. And it was Erev Yom Kippur, a day before Yom Kippur. And my sister-- she's very religious. And she says, oh, my gosh, we have to work. And we have to eat. And it came out, we have no eat. We have no food, because the kitchen wasn't ready. Nothing was ready. They bring us-- they brought us in a camp. It was nothing. We have-- and next day, they fix everything. And we didn't to work, because they hadn't got their transportations, and they haven't got the food to give us. So we got Yom Kippur. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how you had Yom Kippur off.

speaker: interviewee
text: Really, Yom Kippur-- it was fasting, and we wasn't working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Now, when you were taken to Bremen, the purpose of the camp was a labor camp. And what were the girls supposed to be doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we work on the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the city--

speaker: interviewee
text: In this city, on the street, because it was a lot bombarding. Because it's a port city. It's the-- bombs was falling like snow. We was working on the street, and we cleaned up the streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was heavy labor, moving the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Labor, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --parts of buildings that were bombed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. And we were staying on the street and working. And we got a yellow-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] star on the front and a number and the same in the back. And one sleeve was black, and one sleeve was red to see us from the far. Because at first, no-- it would be toilets, nothing. If you need to go to the toilet, you have to ask the SS men. And you went to a bombard house, what is in-- you know, after the bombarding. And we clean up ourselves. And in the meantime, we run across, and we begged. The German people passed through. And we begged, maybe you have something for us. And they didn't know what kind of people we are. They didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And sometimes they passed through, they asked the SS men, what kind of people are there? They're staying. They're singing opera songs. They think that-- they look to me normal. So they says, they're Jew-- that's Jude. So as you worked--

speaker: interviewee
text: When they hear Jude, they run away, because it was not allowed to speak to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And meanwhile, had any of them ever given you anything when you asked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they give us-- they give us bread. They took lunch to work, and they give it to us. They give us money. We went into the bakery store. But the SS men was in the other side. They didn't see it. And they gave us bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were so nice to us, the Bremen people. I have to tell you something. My cousin was working on the street. And a lady passed through. She says, are you hungry? She says yes, I'm hungry. She says, would you like to come to my house with me? So she took a chance. She went with her to her house. And she give her soup to eat, and she give her bread. She says, you have somebody with you? She says, yes. I have a sister. The sister was very young. And in the meantime, from the other room came out SS man in a brown uniform.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was the [INAUDIBLE] worst. He was a young-- maybe 23, 24 years old. And my cousin almost fainted. She was sure he's going to kill her. And she says, don't get scared. He's not going to hurt you. She came out. And she brought a lot of food for all of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the people there actually tried to help a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Especially in Bremen-- I don't know in other cities. Now, in Bremen, they have the lunch. And when we begged them-- we speak perfect German that time-- and they give us the bread, the-- everything. They give us card for bread to buy in store.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were singing while you worked? You were singing opera?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were singing opera songs, everything on the street. And they were staring, and they said, oh, gosh, what kind of people are they? And they asked the Germans. And they says, they are Jude. Whoa-- they have to run away because they were scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not allowed to speak to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: --if a bomb was falling while you were there working in the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us in a-- in underground, in a special place. And the French soldier-- the Jewish-- they was with us in that time. There was a lot of people. And we was holding the door. And it was so strong, the bombs has so much power, we was a few hundred girls and men, and we was holding the door. It was impossible. It was impossible because they give so much power. I don't know-- the first time in my life-- and the door got opened. Nobody can help.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were with French prisoners of war who are also working there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They was on the street. And when the alarm came, all of us ran to the-- it was-- they called a-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: A shelter, like a bomb shelter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a bomb shelter. Now in German, I forgot how they called it. I forgot a lot-- I used to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people that lived in Bremen must have been spending a lot of time in bomb shelters, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, yes. Now, Hitler was never there. He was scared to come to Bremen. They would killed him, because it was only marines. It was a port-- a port city, beautiful port city.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did these French prisoners of war treat the Jewish girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they-- we know they are Jewish. And they know we are Jewish. They don't know they are Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans don't know they're Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. They don't know. They throw away the passports. When they don't have the passports-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's it. It was 26 from them. And they help us a lot. They bought me shoes, and they brought bread. They was free, because they didn't know they are Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you remained there doing this work for nine months.

speaker: interviewee
text: For nine months.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to this other camp, then they would truck you in to do the cleaning during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they took us every day to Bremen. Now, it was in Bremen impossible to come in. They send us on farms to work. And on a farm fall a bomb. The bomb was-- I don't know how to-- it was double like this house. And it didn't blow up. We was sitting over there. But there was no bunker, what they called bunker in German-- a bunker. So there was no bunker. We were sitting, and we run the side. And the bomb was laying and doesn't blow up. I'm telling you, I don't know-- and lot of things, you have to believe-- it's amazing-- amazing. And after, they didn't send us anymore. Because it was short, short. So they send us-- they took us to pick up the other girls. They was a working in a-- in a small town in a dairy factory-- the Hungarian girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we have to pick them up. And we pass a bridge. And there wasn't guarantee, because it was mines. And we have to run in ten minutes. It was 300 girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- we passed through. In a few minutes-- boom. What a beautiful city-- oh, it's so gorgeous. We wish we were dogs, and we can run-- not human being, no dogs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then where did they take you from Bremen?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Bremen to Bergen-Belsen. And we got to there-- the eldest, I mean, he took care in the camp. He was unbelievable-- such a nice person. Two [LAUGHS] girls, they give birth. Hungarian girls-- they came pregnant. And he was staying outside. They had the front and the back like that. Everything should be fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bremen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, in Bremen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And the children remained in Bremen in camp with us until the [GERMAN] man came. And he looked around. He says, what is here, a kindergarten? And they sent them right away to Bergen-Belsen. The girls--

speaker: interviewer
text: The mothers too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the girls with the children. We met them in Bergen-Belsen. They took away the children, and we met the girls. And they kept them in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one of the people that was running the camp in Bremen, was he a German, a nice person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a-- a older man, and he was feeling sorry for us. He tried the best for us. When we left Bremen, how much bread he got, shoes, he give it to us. He says, listen, children, wouldn't take long. To the eight days, all of you is going to be free-- [? end ?] of the week. No, we came to camp with bread. It was a lot of gypsy girls. And they took away everything from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this man, the nicer one that gave you-- do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The nicer one, I don't remember the name. No, the French-- they came after the war, and they killed him, the-- the-- the 26 Jewish boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: They killed him?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't know he was so nice to us. And they killed him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we heard about that, we wouldn't let them. He says, I feel with you, because I was in the first World War. And I was in Poland, he says. And I haven't got food. And we ate what the horses-- the shit from the horses, he says. And I feel. I wish I can do more for-- for all of you. It was unbelievable. You know, what's is true is true.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So then how did they get you from Bremen to Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, by open trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Part of it you had to walk, and then you got into trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. I have to pass the bridge, which was with mines already. They would blow up in a few minutes. And-- and then, we came to Bergen-Belsen. It was no place. All of us they lay on the floor. And the lice on the wall was so big. They ate us alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you hadn't had this problem in Bremen.

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- oh, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had been able to keep clean.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bremen, they took us every week to make showers. And we washed the laundry by ourselves. It was allowed to bring in water. And we have stoves inside. And we smuggle food. And we cook inside. [LAUGHS] It wasn't so bad. We got the best camp. What I hear from other girls, we was in a paradise.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then Bergen-Belsen was--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Bergen-Belsen--

speaker: interviewer
text: --terrible that week.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you any--

speaker: interviewee
text: We was laying on the floors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any food there at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: They bring food. No, it didn't come to us. Because the gypsies was before. The Russian girls was before. And they catched everything. We brought bread for the-- he gave us from Bremen, shoes. They took everything away from us. And we was laying on the floor until they-- the English soldier came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was about a week after you arrived.

speaker: interviewee
text: A week after we arrived. Exactly, he says, children, hold. Hold. Keep on. Try your best. To the week, you're going to be free, all of you. And that was later.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the liberation like? Did you see the British soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the liberation. It was-- it was the Russian girls with us. It was a lot of French girls with us. And everybody sang the hymn-- Polish girls, the hymn, Russian girls, the hymn. It was maybe 1,500 girls in-- in-- in one hall. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- we wasn't singing. We was crying. It was unbelievable-- the wait after the six years, to pass through so much, and all of a sudden--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of-- well, how was your health when--

speaker: interviewee
text: My health was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --at that point with the lice and no food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a lot of people, they got typhus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, from the hundreds. And I didn't catch, and not my sister. And my cousin-- her sister, she got typhus and another girlfriend of mine we know from the Lodz ghetto. Because her father was my foreman in the sewing-- in the factory where I was sewing. That's why I get protection, all of it. I could get any job, because I got a lot people with-- from high-- you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So then after the liberation, you stayed in Bergen-Belsen for while?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was in Bergen-Belsen. After they cleaned up the barracks from the German soldier, and they bring us to Bergen-Belsen. So we was also six or seven girls in one room. One lives-- two is in Washington. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one was a daughter and a mother. She went back to Poland with her daughter. She got a lot of money in the-- in the-- in-- in the-- and she went back to find the money. It was maybe eight girls in one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people start trying to find out what happened to the rest of their families?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and three girls in Toronto. And two died already. And one daughter, she has a show on television-- Shirley-- a talk show in Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the girls were staying together for a while after the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We have no place where to go. And we came to Bergen-Belsen. They start to go to Poland. They start to go to look--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people hear about-- were people beginning to find out what happened to their relatives, who was alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: They want to find out. That's why their-- that's why my brother came to the Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: To try to find out.

speaker: interviewee
text: To find out, because they let them know-- my cousin. They came last week from Israel. That's the cousin. He came to my sister's party, especially. So they find out he lives and his sister. And in the meantime, in the synagogue, on Yom Kippur, he find a friend of mine. He was my boyfriend before the war. He was in love with me. And I wasn't-- and I-- and I didn't want to hear him, to listen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he find me after the war. And he want to marry me. And four weeks before the wedding, I says, listen, you're not going to be happy with me. Because I can't love you. And better clean up, and that's it. So he find me. He was looking. And it was-- in Bergen-Belsen was the people, what all they remain alive, was the names. So he find me in Bergen-Belsen. And he picked me up. And he brought me to Lindenberg. And I lived in Linderberg. And when I get married, I got my own room. How did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my husband-- [LAUGHS] My husband was with three friends in Russia in the army, in camp. And they lived in Bergen-Belsen, all three together. In that time, there wasn't-- none of them was married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my brother-in-law-- it's some relatives-- I think she was a relative. And he asked me if-- she needs a dentist, and in Bergen-Belsen was no dentist-- so if she can come to Lindenberg and stay with me. I says, why not? She can stay with me. I was with my brother at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when she went back, she says to her-- to her brother, I met a girl, and I want you should see her. I don't know what she says. Maybe I'm good. I don't-- I don't know. I didn't hear.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the three friends, they came to see me. You see the picture over there? When I-- I went to pick them up from the train. And they went to a hotel. And we spent a few days. I went to eat there one day. And that's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: They went back to Bergen-Belsen. And she says, my brother, he want to marry you. He fell in love with you. I says, no way. I'm not getting married. I have time. And I'm not-- I wait for my husband. I didn't want to listen. They live in Washington. I met-- I was in Washington to visit them. And they-- he says-- and he was married already. He says, listen. You're not going out-- you're not going out from our family. I am married. And you have to marry my friend. And the friend was my husband. And I didn't want it, either. And they says, you're going to miss a lot. He's very intelligent. He's really out of this world. So I married him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were still living in Lindenberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bergen-Belsen we married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you married him in Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the date of your marriage?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date-- on the-- January 6 is our anniversary. It's going to be--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's going to be 47 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter is 46 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were married in 1948?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, I didn't-- I remembered--

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you decide then to go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my husband, he didn't want to go no place. Because he was before the war in Israel with his wife. And his wife got sick. And she went back to Poland. That's why he run after her. And he got in trouble. And he loved Israel. He was staying in Tel Aviv with the Byks, with the-- the-- the two-- because they were scared for the Arabs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went back?

speaker: interviewee
text: He wouldn't go no place. My sister went to America because my brother-in-law, he got the whole family here. And-- and he-- and after, in Israel was very bad. So I forced him to leave Israel. It was very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in Israel for a few years.

speaker: interviewee
text: For four years, and my daughter was born there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the conditions that were bad that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Bad, you know-- it wasn't-- you got three eggs a week. We give it to the child. You got one portion of chicken, one week. It was very, very little. And the rest, you have to buy on black market. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And to get a job, you have to-- you have to know the president. If not, you couldn't get a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you decided to--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: --live in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and so I decided, because we lived with my cousin together. There went away. And I says-- and his third friend, one lives in Washington. And my husband-- and the third one lives in Toronto. And my friend with her daughter is in-- has a talk show, Shirley, she put-- they got relatives-- Canadian born. They was in cow business.

speaker: interviewer
text: So his friend maked us the papers. And they-- and she-- and they need somebody to guarantee for us, that we-- you're not going to fall for the government. The government should support us.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says to her cousin-- he was Canadian. She says, listen, you have to do it. That's my best friend. When I was sick, and I was laying with hay fever-- with high fever, high, high, in the [NON-ENGLISH], in-- in the sick room, and she gave her bread. People stole the bread from me. I got under my pillow. And she brought bread, what she got. And she give me in-- in her-- in my mouth. And thanks to her, I am alive. They came to my daughter's wedding. I went to her daughter's wedding. We are very close. And you have to do for her, and you have to guarantee. And they guarantee. I never met the people. Because we came to Montreal, because my cousin was in Montreal. And it was four weeks before the-- before Pesach. And we got tickets to go to Toronto. And I says to my husband, you know, over there, it's very close friends-- very good. You know, that's my cousin. And with her, I was in concentration camp. And we slept one bed up on the other. And we are very close. And we live in Israel four years in the same apartment-- same rooms. She got one bedroom. I got one bedroom. And we got a little dining room, the same kitchen, the same bathroom. And we very close. And I says, I didn't want to move to Toronto. I want to stay with them. And now, she was mad. When they-- when they came out, the whole trouble, she says, you see, when you go to Toronto, we will run after you. So you remain by us. That's why we are staying in Montreal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's how you lived in Montreal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, because of them. And they stay because of us. When we go to Toronto, she says she will come after us. And the referendum, everything-- she says, thanks to you, we got stuck here. And I says, thanks to you, we got stuck here.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just wanted to go back a little bit. After the war, when you were finding out what happened to people, did you hear about what happened to Rumkowski? When he-- he was deported when you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we find out they burned him alive. They didn't put him in crematorium. Because it was a lot of people from the Lodz ghetto. And they done that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he went out in August '44 when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife and his brother-- yeah. He-- he wasn't in the same transport. No, he went with us because it was finished, the liquidation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who-- who-- the people got him from Lodz, and they did this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They-- they says, nobody's going to take care on him. We're taking care. Because he took care for us for so many years. When the German want, let's say, 1,000 people, he give 2,000 to give a choice-- always like that. It could be a lot of peoples be saved. And they was very mad at him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took him, his brother, his sister-in-law, and his wife, all four. That took-- they took alive. They burned him alive. That's what I hear. I wasn't there, no-- what I hear. And you heard this from people who were in Auschwitz with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: From people, yeah. Oh, my brother-in-law, he was in Auschwitz for so many years. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So meanwhile, everyone that you knew in your family that survived, you we were able to keep in touch with and find after the war. But you-- did you ever hear anything about your first husband, what had happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Never had a word from him.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And I haven't got the-- from his sisters, I haven't got the address, nothing. Who was thinking a thing like that? So it's gone-- gone with the wind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And so you moved to Montreal with your husband and your daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, from Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Israel. And what work did the two of you do in Montreal?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was working in a factory for ladies coats and suits. And my husband was-- he was a bookkeeper from home. He was working in a bank, in a very big bank in-- in Warsaw. And he got a job through a-- a relative of mine. He wouldn't go and beg for a job. He was too shy and we were too proud. So he get a-- a-- a shipper in a manufacturing factory-- underwear. And he was working the whole time till he retired-- never unemployment insurance, nothing. He was the head shipper. He sent out for $100,000 merchandise every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now he's in a nursing home in Montreal?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's in a nursing home. Yeah, he's 91 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: 91 years old. And your daughter has two children.

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter has two children.
